5:3:
e live in a time when almost everything can be 
anticipated and organized by artificial intel-
ligence. Perhaps for that reason, what we miss most 
is precisely what escapes any system: 
presence. In this edition, our curation begins with a simple 
reflection. What remains of a journey when 
the essential cannot be automated? The future is hu-
man. Technology eases the way, optimizes choices, sug-
gests possibilities. But luxury, in the end, lies in being under-
stood. In having someone who knows you and translates what 
you most desire into the shape of a journey.
5:4:
Sometimes the desire is for journeys that take us 
far away. Other times, for those that take us within. And there are those 
rarer journeys that do both at 
once. That is the encounter we seek here. 
Between extremes, contrasts and worlds that, at first 
glance, do not speak to one another, but which, when lived 
with presence (with what escapes any 
system...), reveal the same essence: the 
capacity to transform us.
5:5:
From the silence of Namibia, where time seems suspended 
and emptiness takes on meaning, to the vibrant landscapes of 
Morocco, where every sense awakens almost 
at once, we continue to experience destinations that
5:6:
impress, and that endure. Places where 
the experience goes beyond what the eye sees and settles inside 
those who live it. From the most impressive national parks 
of the United States, where nature reveals itself on 
an almost unbelievable scale, to the Greek islands, where 
culture, history and beauty still converse. Passing, 
farther north, through Lapland, which invites us to 
contemplation among sleds and reindeer. Different rhythms, 
different geographies, one same starting point: 
that of looking at the world with greater attention.
 
And, because luxury also lies in motion, we sail 
through a new era of travel on the high seas. New 
yachts are redesigning the concept of hospitality, such as 
the newly launched Four Seasons Yacht and the eagerly awaited 
Orient Express Corinthian. We also follow the creative 
pulse of the world, with a global circuit of art and 
design that turns cities into living stages of 
contemporary expression. To travel, today, is also to 
let yourself be crossed by new ideas, new aesthetics, new 
ways of seeing. To understand the impact of our choices. 
To seek experiences that not only enchant, but that 
also respect, preserve and leave something positive 
wherever they go. And you need not even choose between 
desert, savannah or sea. Between silence and movement. 
Because you can allow yourself to live it all.

I 
t's the Brazilian dream! Joy, ginga, grit, color. Style, 
high spirits, competence, celebration. Lightness, ancestry, bold-
ness, samba no pé. Brazil has a certain something we can 
call by many names, but they all lead to the same re-
sult: we are intriguing the foreign gaze, and now they 
want to know. What is it that Brazilians have?
9:5:
Well, beyond outrageous natural beauty, an enviable cui-
sine and a deeply rich culture, we also have plenty of in-
ternational travelers landing on our soil. 
We broke every record and welcomed 9.3 million of them 
last year — a milestone celebrated in true Brazilian style, 
in Copacabana to the beat of Mangueira's drum line.
9:6:
As a result, our country had the largest increase in foreign 
tourism in 2025: 37.1% compared to 2024. By comparison, 
second-place Egypt (p. 240) grew 20%. Another survey 
revealed that, already in January of this year, Rio de Janeiro (in 3rd 
place) and Florianópolis (in 8th) were among the most-booked 
flight destinations on the planet. We are on fire!
9:7:
We are the ones who profit: beyond the injection of billions into our 
economy, we are gaining excellent lodging options (among them, 
the most celebrated was perhaps the 
Four Seasons Hotel Rio de Janeiro at Leblon). The arrival of 
some is still in the rumor or promise stage. Good 
examples are two brands focused on nature lodges 
and experiences: Beckons, a merger between Baillie Lodges and Tierra 
Hotels, and Awasi (we actually think both would be 
perfect fits with our territory). But plenty of news is already 
confirmed in every region of the country. Only our rich 
Central-West was left out — hello, investors! Read on next 
about some of the most high-end options.

• NORTHEAST
The beaches of our Northeast are the perfect spot for barefoot 
projects — and hoteliers know it. We can single out Alagoas 
as the current muse of this market; in particular, the region of the 
Rota Ecológica dos Milagres. The NANNAI MILAGRES, the brand's third property, 
is among the most anticipated openings of the year there. 
It sits on Praia do Marceneiro, in Passo de Camaragibe, and is already taking 
bookings. ORIGEM PATACHO, already open, and SALG PATACHO, slated 
for 2027, are two new names on Praia do Patacho, in 
Porto de Pedras (AL). At the design-hotel Origem, the villas with private 
pools caught our eye; at Salg, the all-inclusive concept 
(with six dining venues) within a 120-key 
layout. Tailoring master Ricardo Almeida will bring 
his style to the state with HOTEL RICARDO ALMEIDA, alongside a 
beach club, a condominium and a farm. The sketch on the next 
page is his — it's the Brazilian National Team's uniform for this year's 
world cups. Chic.
10:13: AVENTORA RESORT will be located in Rio Grande do Norte
10:14:
BAÍA FORMOSA, MINOR RESERVE COLLECTION, due to arrive 
in 2028 with the option of private residences and plenty of water 
sports (even kayaking through the mangroves). Another state still in 
is Ceará (and its kitesurfing), which has become the summer home of many 
Paulistas. An opening is planned there — perhaps still this 
year — for CARMEL ICARAIZINHO RESORT, the group's fourth property (p.216), 
which will bring João Armentano's architecture and the Caudalie spa to the 
village of Icaraí de Amontada. Meanwhile Preá, 15 minutes from the

the Rancho do Kite school on site, it will be a new point for kitesurfers 
of the sport. The group also plans to debut this year, in 
Bahia, the ANANTARA MAMUCABO BAHIA RESORT, for dune rides 
and humpback whale watching.

ne of the most talked-about options in Brazilian 
hospitality in recent years. About an hour by car

14:10:
Good news for São Paulo fans: the city will gain 
two street cinemas. The reopening of Cine Copan, after 40 years 
abandoned, will take place in 2027 following an extensive renovation 
and is expected to feature about 440 seats, a 17-meter-wide LED screen and 
a Dolby Atmos sound system. Cine Tutu has also entered the pipeline. 
In Vila Madalena, the project will seat 114, with 
architecture by Isay Weinfeld and a branch of the Barouche café-bar. And it is not only 
in Brazil that street cinemas, a nearly extinct culture, 
seem eager to return to city life; earlier this year,  
Berlin celebrated the reopening of the Soviet-era Kino International.
14:12:
Trademarks of Cappadocia, fairy chimneys — 
rare geological formations sculpted by water 
and wind — have been found for the first time in 
Brazil, in the northeast of Goiás. Deep in the Cerrado, these 
towers of up to three meters in height will have visits 
capped at 40 people per day to ensure their 
preservation and promise to boost travel to 
Campos Belos, between the Chapada dos Veadeiros and 
the Terra Ronca State Park. On that note: it is worth 
noting that Embratur has launched a digital portal to 
promote Brazilian parks and sustainable tourism. 
If you'd like to take a look, the address is below.

14:19: The HYPE | W E  L O V E  N E W S
14:21:
Stretching from Chicago to Santa Monica, Route 66 prepares 
for its centennial in 2026. Along 3,940 km, 
nostalgic towns form the apex of U.S. road-trip 
culture. Highlights include Pontiac (where former 
president Lincoln lived, in Illinois), Shamrock (an art deco gem with 
Old West roots, in Texas), Tucumcari (awash in neon signs, 
in New Mexico) and Albuquerque — setting of the series 
Breaking Bad, the city commissioned artists to create large-scale 
installations honoring the road's one hundred years. 
Adventurers can travel in local style aboard Airstream's iconic 
aluminum trailers; the company backs the campaign 
to designate Route 66 as a National Historic Trail.

by Marina Gouvêa
 The future
  is human
43:6:
A personal manifesto on technology, 
sensitivity and choices that matter
44:3: Planet | P U R P O S E
44:4:
We live in a time of extreme acceleration. 
Technology advances, artificial intelligence 
improves, systems become ever more 
efficient. Everything is faster, more precise, more 
scalable. And yet, I have never seen so many people 
tired, anxious and in search of reconnection.
44:5:
It is precisely for this reason that I believe the fu-
ture of luxury tourism will inevitably be 
more human.
44:6:
Luxury was never about  
going farther, it was always  
about going deeper
44:7:
Over decades working with high-end  
travel, I have learned one truth:
44:8:
Not because technology has failed – 
but because it has made clear,  
with clarity, that which it  
is not capable of replacing
45:2:
Encounters.  
Real connections.  
Journeys that 
linger.
45:3: Planet | P U R P O S E
46:3:
I use technology every day. I believe in it. I invest in it. It 
helps us personalize, anticipate desires, eliminate friction and 
gain time. A lot of time. And time, today, is the greatest luxury of all.
46:4:
But no database interprets a silence. No 
algorithm understands a moment of life. No automation 
perceives when a trip is not just leisure — it is healing, cele-
bration, transition or new beginning. Luxury begins precisely there.
46:5:
The new luxury traveler seeks relevance, not excess. They remain 
demanding, sophisticated, well-informed. But now they want more than 
access. They want, precisely, meaning.
46:6:
They want to understand the impact of their choices. To travel with con-
sciousness, respecting cultures and people. They want experiences that 
dialogue with their personal history and with the world around them.
46:7:
This traveler does not want to be served. 
They want to be understood.
47:2: Planet | P U R P O S E
47:3:
...although it is a trend, it is, primar-
ily, a stance. Technology, for me, only 
has value when it frees space for the essential: 
the human relationship. When it lifts the weight of the opera-
tional and returns focus to listening, to 
care, to curation done with intention, to 
experiences designed with sensitivity.
47:4:
The true luxury of the future will be made of intelligent 
choices. Of genuine encounters between people, 
places and stories. It will be precise and personalized. The 
travel consultant of the future, more than selling itin-
eraries, will be an expert in the translation of desires.
47:5:
I see the future of luxury tourism in the hands of pro-
fessionals who know how to read people, not just 
maps. Who understand that every trip carries 
invisible expectations.
47:7:
Who know when 
to surprise and when 
to simply respect.
48:2: Planet | P U R P O S E
48:3:
A luxury that is felt. One measured in memories, more than 
in stars; a luxury that does not flaunt itself. That is why I believe 
the future is not about doing more, it is about doing better.
48:4:
I believe in a tourism where innovation and empathy walk 
together. Where artificial intelligence enhances care, 
but never replaces the human. Where every journey leaves 
something positive — in those who travel and those who host.
48:5:
This is the luxury
that endures
48:6:
Because, in the end,  
it is not destinations that 
transform people.  
It is the experiences  
lived with intention  
and humanity. And that,  
for me, always was, and  
always will be, the true  
meaning of luxury.

51:2: Planet | N A M I B I A
51:4:
Namibia is a territory that precedes narratives. Before the geopolitical decisions that 
established borders and maps, there already existed the desert, the wind, and the ancestral relationship 
between man and scarcity. It is one of the youngest countries in Africa in political terms, 
having gained independence in 1990, but its human history goes back millennia, 
marked by the presence of the San, Nama, Damara and Himba peoples, who learned to inhabit 
one of the most unforgiving geographies on the planet.
51:5:
German colonization in the late 19th century left deep marks, both on the architecture 
and on collective memory, and was followed by decades of South African administration under the 
apartheid regime. Independence, won after a long diplomatic and 
resistance process, gave rise to a nation that today stands out for its political stability, its 
commitment to environmental conservation and a low-impact tourism model, 
where exclusivity and preservation walk hand in hand.
51:6:
Traveling through Namibia is a process that does not suit every kind of traveler. One must 
accept long distances and solitary roads. The absences are many. Absence 
of crowds, of noise, of urgency. Namibia has one of the lowest population densities 
in the world, with roughly three people per square kilometer. An essential 
and silent Africa.
52:2: Planet | N A M I B I A
52:3:
Windhoek, between past and present
The gateway to my adventures in the country was Windhoek, 
the Namibian capital, functional, airy and without the chaos that usually 
marks many African capitals. The German heritage is visible 
in the architecture, the cafés and even the beer culture. The unmissable 
Independence Memorial Museum offers a clear and necessary reading of the 
struggle for national autonomy, giving the visitor context 
on the still-recent wounds of the country's history and 
enabling a local immersion I deeply value. From the capital 
begins the crossing through the interior, where asphalt soon gives way 
to gravel roads and space begins to expand 
almost vertiginously.
52:4:
shifted, the lake dried up, the camelthorn acacias died, and the 
desert took definitive control of the space. The near-absolute 
absence of humidity preserved the black trunks, which 
neither rotted nor decomposed. They remain there, 
motionless, like natural sculptures drawn by time.
52:5:
Entering Dead Vlei is like crossing an invisible threshold. The 
visual contrast is so striking that it seems artificially 
constructed, something born from artistic minds. The white ground 
reflects light with intensity, the sky takes on a deep, 
uniform blue, and the orange dunes rise around 
like monumental walls. At the center, the fossilized 
trees create a graphic composition of rare aesthetic force. 
No image, however iconic, prepares the 
visitor for the scale and clarity of this scene, for the impact 
that only those who see with their own eyes can feel.
52:6:
The light at Dead Vlei is an essential narrative element. In the 
early hours of the morning, the shadows cast by the 
trunks lengthen across the pale ground, creating a graphic 
play that shifts with every instant. As the day goes on, the high 
sun flattens volumes and makes the landscape even more abstract, 
almost minimalist. More than a tourist spot, 
Dead Vlei functions as a synthesis of Namibia. There 
is no exuberance in the classical sense, nor apparent 
diversity. There is rigor and restraint. The gemsbok, which inhabits it, 
is like a symbol of that restraint: it has adapted to live 
on little water. (It is also a symbol of the country, which holds 
the largest population of the species and features it on its 
coat of arms).
52:7:
Walking among the trunks is an almost meditative 
exercise in contemplation. There are no sounds beyond one's own step 
on the hardened clay. There is no perceptible wind. Time 
seems suspended, and the notion of the present dissolves. For 
adventurers, the climb to the valley's tallest dune at 320 
meters, Big Daddy, is a serious challenge and a demanding physical 
feat, but from the top the view vanishes into a sea of rippling 
sand, where the sense of scale disappears.
52:8:
On leaving Dead Vlei, the sensation is one of inner 
displacement. A kind of sensory shift that accompanies 
the traveler long afterward. In a world increasingly 
saturated with stimuli, Dead Vlei imposes itself as one 
of the rare spaces where emptiness is not absence. To be there 
is to understand the power of permanence in an environment 
where nothing should survive.
52:9:
Dead Vlei, the desert as origin
The Namib Desert 1  is not merely an impressive backdrop 
for posed photos. It is a living entity, considered 
the oldest desert in the world, at around 80 million 
years old. And the chance to live this experience demands 
commitment from the traveler. Before setting foot on the parched ground 
of Dead Vlei, the route crosses tall dunes, usually in 
4x4 vehicles, and ends on foot.
52:10:

ancient clay plain that, about 900 years ago, was fed 
by a branch of the Tsauchab River. When the watercourse

54:1: Planet | N A M I B I A
54:2:
Skeleton Coast, a brutal  
encounter between land and ocean
54:3:
The "Skeleton Coast" does not bear that name by chance: over 
centuries, thanks to a combination of constant fog and treacherous 
currents, the region has caused countless shipwrecks. The 
skeletons of the ships, many still visible, are part of the landscape. 
Rusted, tilted, partially buried along a fierce coastline 
that has never been tamed.
54:4:
One of the most inhospitable regions of the African continent, steeped 
in legends, feared by sailors and avoided by human 
settlements, it announces itself first through a sharp drop in 
temperature, then through the dense mist rising from the Atlantic, and, 
finally, through the sense of isolation. There, the desert does not end 
at the sea. It advances to the water and seems to fight it for every 
inch of territory. The only sounds are the steady rhythm of the 
ocean and the wind blowing without respite.
54:5:
The experience of traversing the Skeleton Coast is marked by 
austerity. There are no vibrant colors nor exuberant scenes. The 
palette is limited to shades of sand, gray, muted blue and milky 
white of the mist. It is precisely this visual restraint that grants 
the place its aesthetic power. Worth noting is the cinematic 
Sandwich Harbour, with its apotheotic dunes. The scene is 
hypnotic. Perfect lines descend to the ocean, as 
if the world had been hand-drawn.
54:6:
Despite its hostile appearance, jackals find food where the 
land seems incapable of sustaining any form of existence. 
In Walvis Bay, the lagoon is a refuge for flamingos and other 
migratory birds. Continuing to Cape Cross, the largest colony of 
Cape fur seals in southern Africa stretches along the coast. The Skeleton 
Coast and its severe beauty invite silent contemplation 
and awareness that the human being plays a secondary role, 
almost irrelevant before the scale and force of the elements.
54:7:
Spitzkoppe, monumentality  
and spirituality
By 4x4, we headed inland. A cluster of granite monoliths 
rises abruptly from the arid desert plain, 
defying all expectation. Spitzkoppe 2  appears on the horizon 
like a mirage of stone. Known as the "Matterhorn 
of Namibia", this geological formation, more than 
120 million years old, impresses not only by its height but 
by the almost spiritual aura enveloping its forms sculpted 
by time. As the sun moves, Spitzkoppe shifts 
personality: striking golden tones at dawn, 
and a deep red as the day bids farewell. In this primitive 
setting, San rock paintings reveal that 
this is a place of ancestral meaning.
54:8:
Etosha, the Namibia of safari
Etosha National Park 3  is one of the great achievements of 
African conservation policy. Its vast central salt pan 
functions as an axis of wildlife, especially during the dry 
season. The open landscape allows precious sightings: 
elephants moving in calm formations, rhinos emerging 
at dusk, lions resting beneath twisted acacias.
54:9:
And cheetahs: Namibia is a safe haven for this endangered 
species. And its open plains are ideal for the fastest 
animals on Earth to reach up to 113 km/h. Etosha offers 
an authentic experience, where the visitor is only a 
temporary observer. [LT]

In 2026, the United States celebrates 250 
years of existence, to be commemorated 
on the 4th of July, the date that marks its 
political independence from the British 
Empire. Our tribute casts a 
look over the vastness of its deepest 
territories. The ancestral land, home 
to astonishing nature that comes from 
long before humans. The West, where the 
descendants of Europeans built their 
stories by the lasso. The heritage and 
legends of the native peoples. The affective 
memory of a people. Among extraordinary 
landscapes, we list two accommodations 
that are perfect starting points for 
the national parks — one brand-new, 
another iconic — as a reference to that 
centuries-old story.

58:4:
The national parks were born of North American pioneering. In 
1872, the geothermal valley of Yellowstone 1  was designated the first 
environmental preservation area in the world. More than a century and a half 
later, the United States count 63 of these territories of breathtaking 
natural beauty, visited by about 87 million people in just the 
last five years. If you were not one of them, perhaps this anniversary 
is the incentive you were missing to tick off your bucket list  
some of the most impressive natural wonders on our planet.
58:5:
GREAT SMOKY MOUNTAINS 2   
North Carolina & Tennessee
58:6:
The most visited park in the country (more than 12 million 
visitors a year) is far from the best known to Brazilians. 
How about something new? Its mountains, including peaks 
above 1,800 meters, frequently dawn wrapped in a 
blue-gray mist, the result of the humidity released by the 
vegetation. It is home to about 1,900 black bears!
59:7:
The driest and hottest region in all of 
the North American continent has recorded 
an incredible 56.7°C, the record for the 
highest temperature on the planet. Vast dunes and salt flats 
shape the ethereal environment that 
attracted prospectors during the Gold 
Rush, in the late 1840s.
59:10:
The “twin parks” shelter groves 
of majestic giant sequoias, 
cousins of cypresses that can 
reach 90 meters in height. Their 
robust reddish trunks 
reach up to 9 meters in diameter 
– similar to the length of two 
cars in a row. Wow.
59:12:
Trees with a twisted appearance and sharp 
leaves, adapted to the aridity of the Mojave 
desert: these are the Joshua trees. It is said that 
they received this name from the Mormons, because their 
spiraled branches would recall the figure of the 
biblical prophet Joshua in prayer.
59:14:
When spring arrives, the waterfalls 
of Yosemite — one of the most famous 
of all the parks – become more 
beautiful with the melting snow. 
The most imposing is Yosemite Fall – 
740 meters of total drop. Allow yourself 
to visit it on a full-moon night: you 
may have the chance to spot a rare 
moonbow. The “firefall” 
effect in the photo alongside happens for 
about two weeks in 
February at Horsetail Fall.
60:8:
Following the cowboy tradition, 
horseback rides are an authentic way to 
explore the landscape dominated by the 
pointed peaks of the Teton Range, 
a mountain chain that rises abruptly 
from the Jackson Hole valley.
60:10:
The most famous geological record 
comprises gorges, rocky 
pinnacles and ravines carved 
mainly by the waters of the 
Colorado River. Originated between 5 and 6 million 
years ago, the canyon impresses with 
1.6 km of depth.
60:12:
Protected by UNESCO, it encompasses 
two active volcanoes, Kīlauea 
and Mauna Loa. Fields of solidified 
lava and vents of volcanic 
gases give it a 
dramatic appearance. In Hawaiian 
culture, it is considered the cradle 
of Pele, the goddess of fire.
61:6: The granite massifs of NORTH CASCADES
61:7:
NATIONAL PARK form rugged terrain. 
Subalpine meadows and more than 300 glaciers 
earn the park the nickname of “North 
American Alps.” Another must-visit in the state: 
the OLYMPIC NATIONAL PARK preserves the wild 
Pacific coast, with pebble beaches and 
dense temperate forests.
61:9:
Land of mountain 
chains sculpted 
by prehistoric 
glaciers. The scenic 
Going-to-
the-Sun road, normally 
open during the summer, 
offers breathtaking 
views along 
80 km of paved road, 
culminating at Logan 
Pass, at 2,025 meters of 
altitude.
61:11:
Yellowstone has rather 
surreal attractions, from vibrantly colored 
geothermal springs to active geysers, such as 
Old Faithful (which erupts 
shooting jets of 40 meters of boiling 
water every one or two hours) and 
Mammoth Hot Springs, with its 
multicolored travertine terraces. Of 
dimensions comparable to those of an American football 
field, the thermal spring GRAND
61:12:
PRISMATIC displays colors produced 
by microorganisms that love 
high temperatures – thermophilic 
bacteria. Yellowish tones 
predominate in the middle bands; 
the oranges and reds, at the farther 
and cooler edges. More 
about the park on page 129.
62:3:
Brown bear  
(ursus arctos)  
with two cubs
63:7:
Prairies, small glaciers and mountain 
lakes (around 150!) contribute 
to the stunning alpine panorama. 
Displaying their curved horns as if 
they were crowns, the bighorn sheep 
reign in the Rocky Mountains.
63:9:
The dunes are the soul of this park, 
and the tallest, Star and Hidden, 
measure 226 meters. There are no defined 
trails; the invitation is to 
venture freely through the  
78 km² of sands.
63:11:
Gathers approximately 40 glaciers, 
canyons carved by time. The 
region warns us about climate 
change: the melting of the 
glaciers has already begun, threatening the rich 
wildlife that moves between ice 
and forests.
63:13:
Its name means “the high one” in 
the Athabaskan languages, a reference to 
the summit of Mount McKinley: 6,190 meters 
above sea level, it is the highest peak 
in North America. It spans more than 2.4 
million hectares of tundra, 
snow-covered mountains, spruce groves... 
inhabited by wolves, grizzly 
bears, rosy finches and wood 
frogs – the only amphibian adapted 
to the subarctic winter.
64:6:
Utah reveals an almost sacred beauty. 
Rising more than 600 meters, 
the sandstone cliffs of Zion 
impressed the Mormon settlers, 
who, in the 19th century, believed they had 
found the Promised Land: Zion, 
a Hebrew term, can be interpreted 
as “sanctuary.” At Bryce Canyon, 
pink cliffs emerge on the horizon 
and the hoodoos, tall and 
irregular pillars of eroded rock.
65:3:
Landing at Bozeman Yellowstone, home to just 
over 55,000 inhabitants, and being welcomed by the 
life-size statue of a bison is a foretaste 
of what lies ahead. Nearby, in Gardiner, is Yellowstone's 
North Entrance (the only one open year- 
round). Created in 1872, the world's first national park 
is today a UNESCO World Heritage Site. 
Crossing its stone gateway — the Roosevelt Arch — is 
like leafing through the genesis of Earth. The region's 
landscape was shaped by volcanic supereruptions 
more than 2 million years ago. Be- 
neath its fields lies a vast caldera, 
50 by 70 km, making it one of the largest 
geothermal hotspots on the planet. In this sanctuary, 
bison (a constant presence since prehistoric 
times), wolves, coyotes, mountain goats and 
grizzly bears reign in one of the last intact ecosystems 
in the Americas. Spanning three states 
— Montana, Idaho and Wyoming — the park is home to 
half of the world's existing geysers. A place 
where time is measured by eruptions and by the 
indomitable temper of nature.
65:9:
With the most beautiful landscapes of the American 
Old West, Montana is more than a destination of 
endless horizons and snow-capped peaks; it is a state 
of mind that invites us to silence the world 
in order to listen to the heart.
65:10:
MONTANA IN WESTERN RHYTHM
Even though it is one of the largest states, Montana is 
one of the least populated in the United States. It has 
more bison and cows than people. Its vastness is 
interrupted only by the mountain ranges 
of the west, which gave rise to its name, of 
Spanish heritage, "montaña". The region nestles 
beneath the "Big Sky", a nickname that aptly describes the 
immensity of a sky that plays between colors and stars 
and, yes, offers the northern lights. As in a film, Montana 
reads as a wild refuge and storehouse of 
stories, combining indigenous roots, a mining 
past and traces of the frontier West, with the serenity 
of prairies and mountain reliefs that seem 
to touch the firmament.
65:11:
The setting, which has already served as backdrop for 
the melancholy A River Runs Through It, starring 
Brad Pitt and directed by Robert Redford; for the vigor 
of Legends of the Fall, also with Brad Pitt and An- 
thony Hopkins; has now become fashionable with 
the drama of the series Yellowstone, with Kevin 
Costner, and its spin-offs. Cradle of the historic Gold 
Rush, which carved out towns and fortunes, today 
Montana pulses, unhurried, between the charm of small 
towns in frontier-West style and the rigor of a 
climate that challenges the senses, capable of reaching 
extremes of -20ºC. A territory where human 
resilience embraces forests, mountains and crystal-clear rivers 
and shares space with abundant wild
life, a reminder of the force of nature.
66:6:
FROM "BIG SKY" TO  
"BIG SKI": ONE&ONLY 
MOONLIGHT BASIN
66:7:
Far from the buzz of the trendy ski resorts, 
a sanctuary emerges where the grandeur of the setting 
merges with discreet sophistication. In Big Sky, 
luxury whispers. The debut of One&Only Moonlight Basin, 
in November 2025, marks the arrival of the 
Asian brand O&O on American soil. Upon setting foot in the resort, 
after driving along winding roads through forests 
and rivers, the impression is one of stepping onto a 
film set worthy of James Bond. Easy to picture 007 
gliding down the mountains with his “Bogner” 
collection. In fact, elegance here is intrinsic.
66:8:
Its 92 accommodations, 73 of them suites in the main 
hotel building and 19 cabins with Jacuzzi and outdoor fireplace, sit 
on a 240-hectare estate and were designed 
by the architecture firm Olson Kundig, 
specialized in residences, precisely to ensure 
the feeling of "being at home". Wood, stone, steel, 
wool, neutral tones, plenty of leather and local craftsmanship reflect 
the region's identity, while large windows 
invite the landscape in. Going further, the cuisine 
is fabulous from breakfast through to dinner 
served at Wildwood restaurant, with ingredients 
that embrace the western spirit. The alchemy extends 
to the restaurant of chef Akira Back, former professional 
snowboard athlete who now performs radical maneuvers 
in Japanese cuisine.
66:9:
With the ski station reached in five minutes 
via the new O&O Gondola, the peak of après-ski unfolds 
around the fireplaces of the Sky Lounge, the hotel's 
common space with impeccable facilities for skiers, 
at the Moonshack, a whisky speakeasy 
hidden in the forest, or at the iconic Dear Jose- 
phine bar, which pays tribute to the Prohibition era of the Old 
West. To care for your well-being, schedule moments 
of self-care at the spa, which is even supplied 
with oxygen, should the altitude weigh on you. Otherwise, let 
the cadence of the seasons set the rhythm. 
One&Only Moonlight Basin is a tribute to Montana's metamor- 
phosis. Whether gliding through Big Sky's powder snow 
in winter or enjoying the trails of Yellowstone bathed 
in summer sun, the journey is 
always magnetic.
67:5:
There is not a right angle, not a monochromatic 
space on the Colorado Plateau, in southern Utah, almost 
on the border with Arizona. There one can understand why 
both Spinoza, the philosopher, and Einstein, the 
physicist, related divinity and nature. The 
forms, the expanse, the palette of color lead to 
wonder: nature in its pure state, dramatic, 
raw, monumental. I, a photographer, guided by 
light, color and shadow, was dazzled.
67:6:
LIGHT AND WIND
Sunlight plays a decisive role in the landscape. 
It is what makes the colors more dramatic 
or cooler. The climate varies a great deal too. If there is 
shade, there is cold. If the blue sky is in view, heat. 
These variations occur many times on a single outing, 
over just a few steps. The wind 
is omnipresent and a central character in the ancestral 
knowledge and stories of the region. The Navajo 
and Hopi read the whimsical terrain like a 
sacred book. Navajo guides share their knowledge, 
legends, and preserve the natural sanctuary. The story 
of Changing Woman, creator of the seasons 
and of harmony with the land, crosses generations. 
They also speak of the Hero Twins, who confronted 
monsters to protect the local people. For 
the Hopi, an indigenous nation, the small iron 
spheres mingled among the sandstones are signs left 
by the ancestors. Science, for its part, offers a 
geological explanation. Between matter and myth lie 
trails used for 8,000 years, caves, cliffs, 
rivers and lakes, a labyrinth that makes explicit the exact 
dimension of the human and of nature.
67:7:
ANTELOPE CANYON
I began my outings with what is considered 
one of the wonders of the world by the demanding 
Condé Nast Traveler magazine (and by me as well!). Walking through 
the narrow, wavy spaces of the rock formations would already 
be incredible, but there is more. There is the play of natural light 
entering through the cracks. The Earth turns, the Sun shifts position, 
the incidence of light changes and the wall moves from yellow 
to orange, then to pink. In some stretches, the walls 
seem to touch. They grow very narrow, even for 
our human smallness, made still smaller in the natural 
scenography that, at times, recalls a womb. 
It is beautiful. Live, exponentially more beautiful than in 
the most perfectly composed image. Many dimensions, 
colors, heights and textures coexist in a labyrinth. 
Those who fly over this canyon see a narrow, sinuous 
path between two immense plateaus.
68:7:
Small and discreet, the town of Page, in Utah, is the 
perfect base for discovering the region. From there depart the outings to 
attractions such as Antelope Canyon, Horseshoe Bend, a 
surreal overlook of the Colorado River in the shape of a perfect 
horseshoe, Lake Powell, Monument Valley, Bryce Canyon, 
Zion, the Grand Canyon, among others. These scenes can 
be experienced on treks, by ATV, by car, on 
horseback, by boat across Lake Powell, on balloon flights, 
helicopter flyovers or by mountain bike. At Amangiri, 
where I stayed, access to the region's experiences is arranged 
by the hotel itself, which designs tailor-made itineraries 
and balances, with precision, adventure and comfort.
68:8:
I always choose horseback riding. The guide arrived mounted on 
a pinto horse. It looked like a scene from a western, and she had 
everything to be a great character. She told me she lived 
in a trailer, with her border collie and her horse. A 
person passionate about the craft and the place, and thus a guide 
well-informed and moved. Anyone who travels knows how 
much these qualities are decisive for the experience. 
We mounted our horses and entered the park in single 
file. A slow, ideal pace, with the beat of the horses' hooves 
like a sound healing, a sonic experience. 
It is no exaggeration to describe the journey as meditative. 
To sense myself amid so much, with that dust slowly 
rising, was truly magical. I run at high speed.
68:9:
My energy is high. There I was not. It was good. I have always associated 
travel with well-being, and for some time now wellness has been 
a trend in the tourism market. Know this: the Colorado 
Plateau is the right place to experience that fine fashion.
68:10:
All the programs have elements that nourish well-be- 
ing: natural beauty, silence, a new relationship with time 
and space. Impossible to stay on autopilot, you come out of it in the 
first minutes.
68:11:
CONFIDENCE AND FREEDOM
I even felt more confident than usual. It all happened 
on the Via Ferrata outing, with vertical routes installed 
on rock walls. The Cave Peak Stairway, for example, has 
120 suspended steps and one of the most impressive views 
in the region. The landscape opens in 360º of pure geography 
and endless blue sky. No climbing expertise is required 
for the outing. Just the essentials: safety 
equipment, respect for the unknown and for nature, and that 
pinch of courage. I was very secure, attached to cables, 
carabiners. The equipment gave me security, the guides 
too. The guide suggested I "let my body fall back for 
a new sensation". I accepted. Freedom defines what I 
felt. I seemed to float in that magical scene. It was special. 
I climbed steep rocks, crossed narrow suspended bridges, 
hundreds of meters above the ground. Upon reaching the overlook, the 
landscape was the final reward.
68:12:
Sailing across Lake Powell, the landscape shifts: we enter 
flooded canyons and beaches accessible only by 
water. Along the way, groups of kayakers passed. I swam 
in extremely clean, truly pure water, and icy cold. The view is 
vibrant: turquoise water, towering walls in ochre and red 
and, high up, far away, the sky and the Sun. The reflection 
on the lake doubles the desert. I floated in the water, 
embraced by a deep silence.
69:8:
It is no accident that many people choose this destina- 
tion in search of balance and connection. I am a yoga person. I can 
guarantee: practicing at daybreak atop a 
plateau is something unparalleled. I did it daily. I also meditated 
at sunset, when the sky takes on an absurd coloring 
in the best Lucy in the Sky with Diamonds style, the song from 
the Beatles' lysergic phase. Imagine a horizon with shades 
of blue and an almost green. Yes, green! Believe it! It is beautiful 
beyond measure. The tip came from the concierge at Amangiri, 
known as the right place for those seeking well-being.
69:9:
The hotel stretches across nearly 250 hectares, at Canyon 
Point, a protected natural area in southern Utah. The archi- 
tecture interprets the geometry of the desert with minimalist 
precision. The project does not dominate the desert: it dissolves into it. 
Concrete and stone dialogue with the sandstone. The central point 
is the pool, which embraces a monumental rock. It has 
become the symbol of the hotel, connecting the project to the 
desert and to the idea of permanence in an extreme territory. 
An exercise in reverence for the place.
69:10:
The 34 suites follow the same essentialist logic. Neutral tones 
echo the natural palette of sand, rock and sage. 
Wide windows frame the valley. Doors open onto 
terraces with fireplaces. Everything so you can see, in comfort, the 
spectacle that night becomes there. The Milky Way cuts across the dark- 
ness. The wind touches the stone. The warm earth breathes.
69:11:
Local cuisine is celebrated. Regional ingredients (seasonal 
vegetables, desert herbs, local meats) are prepared 
with contemporary technique in wood-fired ovens. Nothing 
processed or ultra-processed. At night, tasting menus 
pay tribute to original peoples. The resort hosts gatherings 
with the Navajo Nation. They share stories, music, crafts, 
rituals. It is beautiful. It deserves to be preserved.
69:12:
Ancestral, local culture also shows in the spa 
treatments, with salt and clay from the territory itself. The experience be- 
gins with smell, with hot pear and ginger tea, with the 
aroma of sage in the air. Little by little, body and mind 
slow down until reaching the frequency of the desert.
69:13:
BEYOND GLAMPING
A few minutes away, Camp Sarika rises like a 
small oasis drawn by the desert itself. I felt that the 
word glamping did not capture what I was seeing: pavilions 
of canvas and stone that reinterpret the traditional tent with 
generous dimensions, pure lines and comfort. Each has 
its own heated pool, outdoor shower, terrace with fire- 
place and a horizon all its own, with canyons so silent that 
you can hear the wind changing direction. And now, in 
2026, the Six-Bedroom Villa will open, six suites set 
in 3.6 hectares of untouched landscape, with a central 
pool of 36 meters. The detail: with a private chef.
69:14:
Looking at the pool, the water reflects the sky and, at sun- 
set, the boundaries dissolve. Amangiri means "Peaceful mountain", 
in Sanskrit, and describes what one feels there: a calm that 
comes from the earth. One must marvel the eyes. Yet, the magic 
is also in all that is invisible. But felt.
71:5:
At the base of two gondolas, the family-friendly resort offers ski-in/
ski-out access in the heart of Beaver Creek Village, one of the winter hot points of the 
Vail Valley. The ice skating rink, the sleigh rides and the renowned 
ski and snowboard school are a must for family adventures, 
from the most tranquil to the most radical. In the afternoon, around the hotel's 
fire pit, with the Colorado mountains as backdrop, savoring s'mores (the clas- 
sic biscuits assembled with marshmallow and chocolate) is a delicious local 
tradition — regardless of age! The fun continues with 
the Little Adventurers Hideaway, an experience that transforms the room into 
a charming intimate campsite with tent and sleeping bags 
for the little ones. Recently renovated, the accommodations present 
contrasts of textures with wool, leather and wood details, plus 
an artistic curation that evokes a contemporary alpine aesthetic.
72:3: Horizons | L A P L A N D
72:6: L A P L A N D
72:10:
You can tell the child at your side: here is Santa Claus's home, Santa Claus's 
supermarket, Santa Claus's gift factory and, of course, Santa Claus's post office, where he collects the 
letters (a way of keeping the magic alive, with the cooperation of postal companies from around the world). 
With sleighs, snowy forests and many reindeer, at this destination everything is rather magical.
72:11:
HELSINKI
A good way to start the trip is through Helsinki. The capi- 
tal of Finland reminded me of Russia — in the era of 
the tsars, it was part of the Russian empire and that has remained 
marked in the architecture. A beautiful city with 
a newly inaugurated Waldorf Astoria. It was at the hotel, 
with a private sauna in the bathroom of my room 
(and others at the spa), that I began to learn 
about the Finns' relationship with sauna culture, 
a very natural habit for them. It is an old tradition 
that is even a candidate for UNESCO Intangible 
Heritage of Humanity status! There are 3.3 
million saunas of different styles for just 
5.5 million inhabitants — having one at home is extremely 
common. To us, it sounds funny: you come home from 
work, have dinner and take a little sauna before bed.
72:12:
DO YOU KNOW WHERE THE LETTERS ADDRESSED TO  
SANTA CLAUS AND DROPPED INTO MAILBOXES BY CHILDREN END UP?  
IN THE ICY FINNISH LAPLAND, WHERE HIS "REAL" CITY IS
72:13:
SANTA CLAUS
We flew to Lapland, seeing from above everything 
covered by the white of the snow, and we found a small, 
crowded airport. It was December and everyone 
wanted to visit the Old Man. The name of his official 
city is Rovaniemi and it holds another attraction 
beyond Santa Claus Village: a line drawn 
on the ground marking the beginning of the Arctic 
Polar Circle — the top of the world! The little town is very 
busy at this time, so for those seeking 
a more reserved, peaceful outing (with 
plenty of activity indoors, sheltered 
from the cold) there is also, nearby, the Santa Claus Secret 
Forest, his secret woodland, and SantaPark Arctic 
World. Since we are speaking of magic, Santa 
Claus will be there too, ready to speak with 
each of the visitors. A show by Santa's 
helpers, a little train and yet another post office (just in 
case) round out the Disney-like vibe of the 
experience. Excellent for families staying at the 
Arctic Treehouse, right next door. It is top-notch, 
with its stylish cabins scattered among the 
pines covered in snow.
73:6: Horizons | L A P L A N D
73:7:
Made from the same mold! Around the region you can also 
walk with snowshoes on your feet (harder than 
it looks), ski (just for fun), float in the water (wearing a 
dry suit), stay in an igloo with a glass ceiling (good 
for those who prioritize seeing the northern lights). And return to being a driver, 
this time of a snowmobile — and, best of all, electric. Without 
the smell of fuel and, above all, without harming 
nature. In Finland, sustainability is a very 
important value. And there is a mini version for young visitors!
73:8:
LETTER DELIVERED BY HAND
I was very much impressed by tourism in Lap- 
land. I think they are very well prepared in infrastruc- 
ture and service and that, combined with the (irresistible) appeal 
of Santa Claus, makes me think a trip like this is an excel- 
lent option for bringing children. And worth bringing your inner 
child too: in case you are wondering, yes, I 
also had my personal moment with Santa Claus. And 
I came home with a photo of the two of us and all... [LT]
73:9:
SLED DRIVER
You place one foot on each side of the sled, on a little 
piece of wood that acts as a brake, and off you go down the small 
road. Yes, I drove a sled! I started apprehensive but 
found it super fun, I enjoyed myself. The dogs were agitated and 
wanted by any means to overtake the sled in front, going 
off the track, accelerating. Just the way I like it! Adrena- 
line in the right dose. But the outing, like practically 
everything around here, is entirely suitable for children. They 
just will not drive (and I advise the more uncoordinated 
to prefer the passenger seat too!)
73:10:
AND MORE ADVENTURES IN THE SNOW
Another sled ride I took was pulled by reindeer (Santa's, 
of course). On this one there is no possibility of 
being the driver, but you can interact with them by feed- 
ing them. I heard many interesting stories about this herd 
and its cultural and economic importance for the region. For 
example, they shed the antlers on their heads 
every year and, when the new ones grow, they come back exactly the same.
73:11:
PRIVATE ISLAND
It was precisely an image like that that I saw through my window when 
I woke up. Beautiful and framed by the window, the pine tree looked like a 
painting. But I was at another hotel in the same family, the boutique Aino 
Private Island, romantic, cozy, incredibly charming (and which only accepts child- 
ren in the case of a buyout). And truly private: to reach it, you 
cross a little suspended bridge. An experience apart. It has a 
most charming space just for you to relax and, obviously, saunas. Plus 
a freezing lake so that, after warming the body, you plunge in the open air 
to cool it down. They do it with no trouble at all!
73:12:
ANOTHER TIME
When I was there, it was the height of kaamos, the polar night. The sun never 
rises and the few clear hours of the day give only a whitish 
light to the sky which, the rest of the time, is dark. In principle, great 
for increasing the chance of seeing the northern lights, but not this time. It is 
always a matter of luck whether you see them or not. In my case, I had bad luck: the next day, 
the mist that covered them lifted and they were visible again. It is also very 
cold, the temperature can drop to less than -20ºC (always, 
before the outings, proper clothing is provided, so do not 
worry about that). But everything is so beautiful, so bucolic, strolling through 
the little forest, feeling the beauty and silence... that nothing interferes and everything 
is part of the experience. Besides, every outing is finished in a ca- 
bin with a lit fire and tea fresh off the stove.
74:3: Horizons | G R E E C E
74:7:
IF THE MANY CITY-STATES OF ANCIENT GREECE WERE INDEPENDENT AND 
LIVED IN ALMOST PERMANENT CONFLICT, 3,000 YEARS LATER, GREECE IS 
SYNONYMOUS WITH SEA, CULTURE, GASTRONOMY AND AN ALMOST ARCHETYPAL BEAUTY
74:9:
In peace, from polis to polis, our journey be- 
gins in Athens — cradle of democracy, 
philosophy, theater and the Western arts 
—, passes through Paros, which has been consolidating 
itself as a tranquil and authentic alternative 
among the Greek islands, and ends in Santori- 
ni, destroyed by a great volcanic 
eruption; a tragedy that, paradoxically, gave 
rise to one of the most impressive landscapes 
on the planet.
74:10:
ATHENS
Continuously inhabited for more than 
3,500 years — with its apogee in the 5th century 
BC — Athens has bequeathed us an abundance of 
temples and ruins. Even so, the capital of 
Greece is far from being a museum city. 
Its four hills offer trails and beautiful 
panoramic views, a young population 
connected to the arts, a new generation of luxury hotels, 
internationally awarded bars 
and a vibrant gastronomic scene — 
the fruit of a millennia-old tradition of devotion 
to food as a form of pleasure and of intellectual- 
spiritual communication at the table.
74:11:
It was in Athens that the world ceased to be 
explained only by the gods and came to 
be interpreted by reason, politics and 
aesthetics. One of the most underestimated European 
capitals by travelers, Athens 
deserves more than the two nights that 
Brazilians generally set aside before 
leaving for the arid Cycladic Islands on one 
side of the country or the verdant Ionian 
Islands on the other.
74:12:
The symbolic heart of the city is, naturally, 
the Acropolis, the great rock that 
housed the fortified "high city" of the polis. 
Envisioned by Pericles, the greatest of 
Greek statesmen in history, the Acropolis of Athens 
brings together not only the Parthenon, but also 
the caryatids (support columns in the 
form of female figures) of the Erechtheion and the 
monumental entrance to the complex, the Propylaea.
74:13: T / H / R / E / E /   / T / I / M / E / S
75:2: Horizons | G R E E C E
75:3:
Around the Acropolis are the historic neighborhoods of Plaka and 
Monastiraki. Though crowded and touristy — full of shops 
and restaurants of doubtful taste, with rare exceptions 
cited in the next paragraph —, do not miss walking 
through the curved, narrow lanes of Anafiotika 1 , in Plaka. 
Set on the slopes of the Acropolis and car-free, it 
seems suspended in time, with whitewashed houses, cats every- 
where and longtime residents. A breath of si- 
lence and authenticity far from mass tourism.
75:4:
On the way back to the "city", it is worth stopping for a 
casual lunch: a Greek salad at Lyra, with tables on the 
most charming staircase in Plaka, or traditional Greek 
cuisine at the century-old tavernas Psaras and Stamatopoulos,
75:5:
the latter to the sound of Greek music (unfortunately, a rarity 
in Athens). If the idea is a late lunch at the most 
luxurious hotel in the city, with the most impressive view 
possible of the Acropolis, simply book and walk 
a few minutes to the restaurant atop the recently 
inaugurated The Dolli 2 .
75:6:
Dine at the flagship restaurant of Estiatorio Milos 3  — a suc- 
cess in New York, London and Singapore —, with fish 
and seafood prepared with precision and served in 
an elegant dining room. Or head toward the coast for 
a future-oriented dinner at Delta, the only two-starred 
restaurant in the country. Set within the Stavros Niarchos 
cultural complex — which also houses the Library
75:7:
National and the Opera — and designed by Renzo Piano, 
the glazed hall of Delta opens onto the sea, in the 
region that, in Classical Greece, housed the Piraeus, then the 
largest commercial and military port of the Mediterranean; 
and, today, the largest passenger port in Europe.
75:8:
To understand the ingredients that shaped Greek 
cuisine over the centuries, it is worth visiting the central 
market, the Varvakios Agora. Built at the end of the 19th century 
thanks to the generosity of caviar importer Ioan- 
nis Varvakis, the Varvakios houses Europe's largest fish 
market, counters serving ouzo and mezze 
prepared with ingredients bought right there, 
cafés where you can have a Greek-Turkish coffee and still
75:9:
familiarize yourself with fruits and vegetables that span 
millennia — olives, grapes, figs, capers, fennel, 
purple amaranth.
75:10:
For lovers of fine mixology, there is no shortage of 
stimuli either. Three city bars are on the list of The 
World's 50 Best: Baba au Rum — an institution with 
more than 400 rum labels —, The Bar In Front Of The 
Bar, with a daily menu based on the zero-waste concept, 
and Line 4 , known for its unusually 
fantastic cocktails and its own production of fruit 
wines. All are indoor venues, however; if 
you want an al fresco drink with a view of the illuminated 
Acropolis, the address is the 360 Cocktail Bar.
76:1: Horizons | G R E E C E
76:2:
On the cultural side, a visit to the Acropolis Museum 5  is indis- 
pensable — and should come before the climb to the archaeological site. 
Not only because you can see up close the original caryatids, 
but also the sculptures of the Parthenon frieze, in the 
same sequence, scale and orientation of the temple, visible through 
the large windows. Here, the void left by 75 linear 
meters of the original frieze of about 160 meters, displayed for 
200 years at the British Museum, makes us reflect on the legiti- 
macy of the "custody" of most of the pieces in the great 
museums of London, Paris and New York.
76:3:
Another essential museum is the National Archaeological Museum 6 , 
which, with Cycladic, Minoan and Mycenaean collections, shows how 
"Greece" was born well before the 5th century BC and was always 
connected to other Mediterranean civilizations.
76:4:
For those with more time — or on their second visit 
—, I also suggest the Benaki Museum 7 . Installed in a 
neoclassical mansion that belonged to Antonis Benakis, its 
collection spans millennia: from the prehistoric to the 19th century, 
passing through Byzantine Greece and Greece under Otto- 
man rule (with a very beautiful section of traditional 
clothing). The museum also illuminates the birth of modern 
Greece as a European nation-state (a process in which 
the enfant terrible and British poet Lord Byron played a decisive 
role — and is therefore considered a national hero in the country).
76:5:
Speaking of attire, it is always worth watching the changing of the 
presidential guard 8  in front of the Hellenic Parliament. With 
high white socks, pleated skirts and the iconic leather 
shoes with wool pompoms and soles studded with
76:6:
nails — weighing nearly four kilos —, the hourly ceremony 
is always a spectacle somewhere between the 
military and the performative.
76:7:
THE PARTHENON
The ultimate symbol of classical architecture, no building 
in history has been more copied than the Parthenon 
of Athens 9  — from Wall Street to the British Museum, 
by way of the Madeleine in Paris. Built around 
2,500 years ago in the Doric style with Ionic elements 
(a later, more delicate and ornate style), the temple 
was erected during the rule of Pericles to house the 
colossal statue (known to us today only through ancient 
records) of the goddess of wisdom and war, Athena
76:8:
Parthenos, as part of the larger Acropolis project. Unlike 
the monochromatic image inherited from neoclassicism, 
the Parthenon was polychrome, painted in various 
colors. The work was a technical feat: more than 
100,000 tons of marble were transported over 
more than 15 km and hoisted 157 meters high — and this in the 
5th century BC. Over the centuries, the temple was 
transformed into a church dedicated to the Virgin Mary, a 
mosque during the Ottoman occupation and a fortress 
torn apart in the battles between Turks and Venetians in the 
17th century. Another fascinating curiosity is that almost no 
line of the Parthenon is truly straight: to correct the 
distortions of human perspective, the architects created 
subtle curves and optical “adjustments” that make the building 
visually perfect.
76:9:
▲ Athena Varvakeion, a reduced 
copy of the Athena Parthenos 
of the Parthenon, made by Phidias in the 
3rd century BC and on display at the National 
Archaeological Museum

78:2:
PAROS
If Naoussa has become the charming face of the island — with good 
cafés, restaurants and shops designed to please the international 
traveler —, it is in Parikia, the capital where the ferries arrive, 
that the history and real life of this (still) 
quiet Cycladic island between Mykonos and Santorini lie, just 
40 minutes by direct flight from Athens. Renting a car is 
essential to explore the island — and neighboring Antiparos 
as well. Distances are considerable, taxis are scarce and 
some of the most beautiful beaches remain practically 
wild, without infrastructure. Having a car serves as a 
base, allowing you to discover the island at a relaxed pace.
78:3:
While the most elegant beach restaurant, Faragas 10, lies 
to the south, the most beautiful beaches in Paros are to the north, in 
the Naoussa region — and all are of fine white sand. 
Kolymbithres impresses with its almost lunar granite
78:4:
formations; nearby, Monastiri is part of a natural reserve 
with trails, well worth a full-day visit. West of Naoussa 
lie Santa Maria, a shallow-water bay ideal for 
families, and my favorite beach: the wild Laggeri, with 
vegetation that provides natural shade and a small 
chapel by the sea.
78:5:
The best restaurants are also concentrated in Naoussa. 
Parostià 11 and Siparos combine well-appointed 
settings, delicious food and sea views; Statheros, 
reachable only on foot, occupies a unique location, with 
tables set on the rocks of the small beach and the sea almost 
touching your feet. Another classic specializing in fish and 
seafood is Barbarossa: it sits opposite the chapel of Saint 
Nicholas, with fishing boats alongside and views of 
the ruins of a fortress built by the Venetians
78:6:
over the sea in the 15th century. Between meals, try 
loukoumades — fried dough balls drizzled with honey — at To 
Paradosiako and stop for a coffee at Santé, in the most 
charming little square in Naoussa, beside the shop Handmade in Paros 
and a small Orthodox church.
78:7:
Parikia holds traces of small temples and altars, 
the monumental Byzantine church of Ekatontapiliani and countless 
tiny private chapels — almost always closed — 
built by fishermen in gratitude after 
surviving the hardships of the sea. On your walks through the island, 
observe: over the centuries, blocks and fragments of 
pagan temples were incorporated into houses and churches in 
a process of spolia, visible to this day. In Antiquity, 
Paros was celebrated for its exceptionally 
white and translucent marble. Besides the Venus de Milo and the temple
78:8:
of Apollo at Delphi — considered by the Greeks the “center 
of the universe” —, it is estimated that over 90% of the sculptures 
today on display in the museums of Athens were made 
of Parian marble. And such noble material could 
never be wasted, even with the advent of new religions 
and new colonizers.
78:9:
Paros was a wealthy, cultured city-state integrated into the classical 
world. Like most Greek islands, it passed 
through Roman, Byzantine, Venetian and Ottoman rule until 
being incorporated into the Greek state in 1832. Tourism would only arrive 
in the 1960s and 1970s, when names such as 
Truman Capote, Catherine Deneuve and Marcello Mastroianni 
spent seasons on the island in search of tranquility, 
bringing with them artist and intellectual friends who put 
Paros on the map of sophisticated tourism.
78:15: © COSME, A LUXURY COLLECTION RESORT, PAROS
79:2: Horizons | G R E E C E
79:3:
SANTORINI
Santorini has a unique landscape. The breathtaking view 
over the calm sea conceals one of the most dangerous volcanic 
complexes in the world, largely submerged. It was 
precisely this still-living threat that is responsible for the 
dramatic and dazzling beauty of Santorini. The horseshoe-shaped 
archipelago is the remnant of a caldera, 
the result of one of the largest eruptions ever recorded in 
human history, about 3,600 years ago. The event destroyed 
Akrotiri, one of the most advanced urban centers of the 
pre-classical Mediterranean, and may have contributed to the 
collapse of the Minoan civilization on neighboring Crete. After 
that, Santorini lost political and economic relevance — 
until it was “rediscovered” by tourism in the 20th century.
79:4:
In Oia — pronounced “Ía” —, the most touristy village, 
houses and hotels are carved directly into the volcanic rock 
of these cliffs reaching nearly 400 meters in height. 
Almost every hotel features small pools 
perched over the caldera on the room balconies 
(since they would be visible from the street above, they are 
sometimes covered by charming “caves” that frame 
the sea view; seen from above, they appear as 
white vaults integrated into the architecture).
79:5:
Santorini is not a beach destination, but it is worth visiting its 
“geological” beaches in every color, with even higher 
cliffs that make the landscapes unusual for us Brazilians:
79:6:
Kokkini (red sand and cliffs), Perissa/Perivolos 
(a long stretch of black sand with the best infrastructure and 
loungers — reserve yours at the boutique-hotel Istoria), 
Lefki Ammos (the “white” beach, accessible only by boat) and 
Vlychada, with its wind-carved cliffs.
79:7:
On the dining front, my highlights lie between the villages 
of Imerovigli and Fira: The Athenian House, Throubi, Selene and the 
traditional Aktaion, in the same family for over a 
century. In Oia, amid a glut of uninspiring options, 
two restaurants-with-infinity-views deserve a 
reservation: Lauda 12 and Botrini’s 13, both with terraces facing 
the caldera.
79:8:
To pair with meals, explore the island’s wines produced 
from rare indigenous grapes shaped by the 
volcanic terroir. The white Assyrtiko, high in acidity and with a 
mineral-saline profile, is the great star; the red Mavrotragano 
yields wines with powerful tannins. When choosing, 
go for the island’s most traditional producers: Artemis Karamolegos 
and Gavalas, both open for visits and essential 
for anyone wanting to understand Santorini’s winemaking identity.
79:9:
Finally, a boat ride. At sunset, Santorini reveals it-
self at its finest: monumental, silent and as a 
reminder that any civilization may be interrupted, 
brutally and irreversibly, by nature. [LT]

representative of the andBeyond team. A private 
transfer will take you to Wilson Airport, from where you will depart 
on the flight to the Kichwa Tembo airstrip, in the 
Maasai Mara reserve — the stage of the migration 
of wildebeest and zebras between Kenya and Tanzania. 
A welcome safari will give the real start to your 
journey, right after check-in at the incredible ANDBEYOND 
BATELEUR CAMP 1 .
 DAY 2
Your day may begin early with an unmissable 
safari aboard a hot-air balloon! Watch the first 
rays of sun touch the Mara River and the vast plains 
of the reserve from a privileged angle, enjoying 
a champagne breakfast upon landing. On returning 
to camp, a guided walk around the surroundings and 
another safari will continue the program.

of a native guide, a visit to a traditional village 
can be arranged. Then, you will take part

AN UNPARALLELED POLAR ADVENTURE! Ponant Explorations announced a daring 62-day journey to 
make a full loop around Antarctica starting January 11, 2028. The trip, beginning and ending in Ushuaia, will take 
travelers across nearly 23,000 km of little-accessed coasts — including passage through the Ross Sea, the largest ice plateau 
on the planet. Zodiac boat excursions and diving challenges in icy waters are scheduled. 
Wildlife watching (humpback whales, elephant seals, seals, penguins...) will be another highlight. Divided 
into two itineraries that can be booked individually or together, the expedition will take place aboard the luxury 
icebreaker Le Commandant Charcot, highly focused on sustainability. This style of cruise seems to be becoming 
a trend... for 2029, Crystal Cruises is planning its own circumnavigation. In this case, around Australia!

China, from Ürümqi to Lhasa — in mythical Tibet! Between September 12 and 24, the program includes visits to the silk workshops 
of Hotan and a nighttime tour of the archaeological site of Yotkan, dating back to a lost Buddhist kingdom. Another highlight: the 
ruins of Jiaohe, dating from the 2nd century BC and protected by UNESCO. Part of the journey will be made aboard the new 
Silk Road Express train; it is worth booking the Han Dynasty Suite, a 22.2 m² cabin that includes private guide and driver services.

MALTA’S TRADITIONAL ARCHITECTURE — carved 
from limestone formed millions of years ago from 
marine organisms — tells a story of

Malta and its legacy 
carved in time
99:14:
Mdina
Malta’s former walled capital, 
Mdina still preserves the medieval 
influences of the Norman conquest of 
1091. At the city’s main entrance 
stands Palazzo Vilhena: a French 
Baroque palace from 1726, built 
after a devastating earthquake. Today, 
it houses the National Museum of Natural 
History, with biological collections 
(including a 4,000-year-old 
mummified Nile crocodile!).
99:15:
The Metropolitan Cathedral deserves 
a spot on your itinerary; a work 
by the Maltese architect Lorenzo Gafà and 
built between 1696 and 1705. The project 
preserved and incorporated elements 
of an earlier church, such as the 1495 
baptismal font and the main wooden door 
of 1530. Marked by 
its octagonal dome and the facade 
with Corinthian and composite pilasters, the 
monument displays, inside, more 
than 160 marble tombstones embedded 
in the floor, belonging to the clergy.
99:16:
Valletta
With the arrival of the Knights of Saint 
John in 1530, the capital was moved 
to Valletta. Using the Mannerist style, 
Girolamo Cassar was the architect 
responsible for designing the city’s main 
buildings during that period — 
including the auberges of the military order 
and the impressive St. John’s 
Co-Cathedral. The Co-Cathedral was the official 
church of the Knights. Despite its 
discreet exterior, the interior is richly 
decorated; the Italian artist Mattia Preti 
painted scenes from the life of Saint John 
the Baptist directly on the limestone ceiling. 
The Oratory displays a true treasure: 
two works by the master Caravaggio, The 
Beheading of Saint John the Baptist and Saint 
Jerome Writing.
99:17:
Another architectural highlight is the 
Grand Master’s Palace, from the 
1570s, which served as the center
99:18:
of the Order’s administration. The building 
became the governor’s residence 
during British rule, but 
today houses the office of the Maltese 
president. There are frescoes depicting 
the Great Siege of 1565.
99:19:
One of the most striking examples 
of British influence on local 
architecture is the Mosta Rotunda, 
designed by Giorgio Grognet de 
Vassé and built between 1833 and 1860. 
Its dome is among the largest 
of its kind in Europe (fun fact: 
during World War II, 
a bomb pierced it but did not 
explode!). Beneath the surface of Valletta, 
there is also an underground network 
carved into the limestone; these tunnels, 
which can be visited today, 
were originally used for 
water distribution and, during times 
of conflict, as air-raid shelters.

religious site carved entirely 
from limestone impresses with 
megalithic structures up to 
five meters long 
and over 500 tons. An 
essential visit!

dusk, rugs that look like paintings. Out in the desert, it 
quiets into a clean horizon that returns you to yourself.
 
Smell follows on its own. Spices come: 
saffron, cumin, preserved lemon; the embers of the streets, the 
sweetness of dates, freshly tanned leather (at times too 
intense, not always pleasant). And the most striking aroma, 
permeating everything, the orange blossom, which repeats like an 
invisible and unmistakable signature of Morocco.
 
Touch appears in the details: in the texture of the Berber 
rugs, in the hand-molded ceramics, in the adobe 
walls warmed by the sun. And in the feeling that, if 
we could touch the soul of the country, it would be warm and ancestral.
102:2:
Taste arrives in varied flavors and abundant tables! Tagines 
(amazing!) slow-cooked, breads prepared at dawn, the 
mint tea served with generosity. (In Morocco, 
to eat is to share, and to share is a form of devotion).
102:3:
Hearing may be the sense that leaves the deepest mark. The bustle 
of the souks of Fès. The voices that cross in Arabic, French and 
Berber. Above all, the call to prayer...
102:4:
...And, with that call, comes the intangible “sixth sense” 
awakened by the muezzins from atop the minarets, like an 
invisible line uniting sky and earth. Even for those who do not 
know the language, there is something deeply spiritual in that sound. 
In Morocco, this extrasensory perception arises slowly, 
but, little by little, it brings new meanings to the journey. 
And, if you allow it, something inside you also changes.

103:3: &Experiences | 5  S E N S E S  |  M O R O C C O
103:5:
The country welcomes you even before landing, when through the 
window a mosaic of colors appears, alternating 
between the sea, the imperial walls and the infinity 
of the desert. Casablanca, often seen only 
as a gateway, is an elegant beginning. It is an 
Atlantic, cosmopolitan Morocco, the country’s present, 
where tradition and contemporary architecture share the 
same horizon.
103:6:
ROYAL MANSOUR CASABLANCA 1  is the hotel of the king 
of Morocco, and you can feel it. The historic building, 
impeccably restored in the city’s art deco 
heart, translates the country today with service that anticipates 
everything. Great for spending time without guilt 
between the spa, the indoor pool and the inner courtyard, with a 
date and a mint tea. In the late afternoon, a drink on the 
terrace... and the sea in front. On the first night, there is no 
better debut in Moroccan cuisine than La 
Grande Table Marocaine, at the hotel itself. A dinner 
that already explains the country. Local haute cuisine, live 
traditional music and a stunning view of the 
Hassan II Mosque.
103:7:
The mosque, which I visited afterward, is a masterpiece rising 
above the sea. It almost floats! The world’s tallest minaret 
seems to touch the sky, the cedar-carved ceilings are almost 
like lace, and the polished marble reflects light with 
delicacy. On your day, also include a surprise: the 
Church of Notre-Dame de Lourdes, with its 
monumental stained glass by Gabriel Loire. Blue, red, 
golden light... I recommend lunch at Kyúb, at the FOUR 
SEASONS 2  hotel, with the sound of the sea waves as 
soundtrack. Unpretentious, light, Latin American cuisine 
(yes!), perfect for a break between outings.
103:8:
When night falls, the alleys come alive, and one 
address stands out: Dar Dada, one of the most beloved 
restaurants in the city. The ambiance combines Moroccan tradition 
with contemporary aesthetics. Hanging 
lanterns, typical music, aroma of spices, warm 
colors, authentic food. Closing the day here is closing the 
circle: spirituality, architecture, sea, light, art, flavor. 
All senses activated.
103:10:
The Atlantic is always present, bringing a breeze and 
a soft light to the capital — orderly and very far 
from the intense rhythm of other cities.  The Four Seasons 
Hotel Rabat at Kasr Al Bahr has the same air of calm. 
In this former palace, Moroccan heritage and contemporary 
design meet in ample courtyards and 
gardens, with the sound of the sea in the background. Between the Kasbah 
des Oudaias (white and blue houses facing 
the sea), the Hassan Tower and the Mausoleum of Mohammed V, 
Rabat reveals a refined Morocco.
103:11:
ON THE WAY TO FÈS
A toast to the unexpected
103:12:
The road announces a change of atmosphere. Everything 
transforms, slows down and surprises with an 
unexpected layer: CHÂTEAU ROSLANE 3  appears 
like a well-kept secret. Part of Relais & 
Châteaux, the elegant property has well-tended 
vineyards and a serenity that contrasts with everything 
one imagines about the country. Amid gardens, cellars and 
lunch with a view of the vines, one witnesses a 
discreet dialogue between Moroccan tradition and French 
heritage. The tasting is excellent, and this toast broadens 
one’s view of the country.

104:5:
In the heart of the Medina, RIAD FÈS  4  is one of the most 
incredible places I have ever stayed, hidden among labyrinthine 
alleys, elegant courtyards, fountains and details that 
tell centuries of history. Staying there is living Fès from 
within. At night, going up to the rooftop and watching the medina 
slowly light up is one of those moments that stay with you. 
Distant sounds, pinpoints of light, the call 
to prayer echoing in the distance... The city breathes.
104:6:
A place where time did not pass, it simply 
settled in. From the top of the Borj Fès viewpoint, the scale 
of the medina impresses. A living tangle of 
rooftops and alleys. Down below, life goes on steadily. 
Open workshops, ancient doors, gestures repeated 
for generations. In a traditional ceramics atelier, 
clay is transformed by the hands of artisans who 
master ancestral trades. Mosaics, tiles, 
geometric patterns. All learned, transmitted, 
respected, preserved. In Fès, making is almost  
a ritual.
104:7:
The arches of Bab Boujloud, in blue and green, 
mark the entrance: walking through the medina is 
to accept getting lost with purpose. Dye works, 
weaving shops, blacksmiths, markets seem 
endless. Time to shop, sample 
spices and contemplate. Between one turn and 
another, the tannery appears for the bravest. 
The odor is in no way pleasant, but the experience 
is striking. In the madrasas (traditional 
Islamic schools), spirituality and daily life 
walk together. At night, illuminated and more 
introspective, the medina becomes even more beautiful.
104:8: High up in the city, the cosmopolitan HOTEL SAHRAI
104:9:
5 , for those who prefer a cooler style, offers 
another vantage point. A drink at sunset, 
followed by an al fresco dinner at the singular  
Jungle Restaurant, with the medina at your feet and 
the most contemporary architecture, guarantee a 
memorable contrast.
104:30:
Fès El Bali
Chouwara Tannery, Fès
104:33:
There is a Morocco far from the obvious settings. It is there that 
the Travel Link Foundation operates, with projects focused 
on dignity, autonomy and the future, spanning 
literacy and training for women, support 
and education for children, and medical support, bringing 
basic care to places it often does not reach. 
Nothing is one-off or paternalistic. It is a continuous presence 
with respect and commitment. Your visit will end 
with lunch at the home of a beneficiary couple. Zero 
luxury, great meaning. Simple food, prepared with 
care, served as a gesture of affection. A profound 
encounter impossible to forget.
105:1: &Experiences | 5  S E N S E S  |  M O R O C C O
105:3:
The city is sonorous, colorful. The souks are an immediate 
sensory plunge. Among rugs, perfumes, fabrics, everything  
calls, everything competes for attention. The Ben Youssef 7   
madrasa appears like a breath. Minute carvings and perfect 
zelliges (traditional glazed tiles). Dar El 
Bacha, a historic palace, reinforces this idea of 
Moroccan refinement. In the middle of the medina, a 
surprise: the 1112 Tea House, discreetly hidden, offers 
a delicious tasting of Moroccan teas and sweets. 
A pause to let time pass more slowly.
105:4:
Beyond the traditional, two different experiences. The 
first at Domaine du Rétro, a collection of vintage 
cars; Rolls-Royce, classic Mercedes, models 
that served royal houses and heads of state in the last 
century. After observing, comes the best: a ride 
in one of the cars from the collection. Wind in the face, charm and 
the sensation of traveling, also, through time. The second 
proposes a vintage sidecar ride, with discoveries 
at every turn. Lightness. Fun. Movement.
105:5:
Pause and contemplation await at Jardin 
Majorelle, one of the most enchanting places in 
Marrakech. Created in the 1920s by Jacques 
Majorelle, it mixes botanical species from around the world, 
reflecting pools and that deep, vibrant blue that 
became its signature. Decades later, Yves Saint 
Laurent and Pierre Bergé fell in love with the space, 
restored it and turned it into a true 
sanctuary. Marrakech was the creative “refuge” and source 
of influences that run through YSL’s work. The visit 
is completed by the Berber Museum, with Berber 
antiquities from the designer’s personal collection, and the Yves 
Saint Laurent Museum, an impressive selection of pieces and 
sketches. Haute couture with identity and context.
105:6:
At night, Marrakech pulses. Jemaa el-Fna square 
transforms into a spectacle with snake 
charmers, dances, olive tastings, in a 
beautifully choreographed chaos that can end 
with a carriage ride back to the hotel. On this 
trip, I tried three hotels, each proposing 
different ways of experiencing the destination.
105:7:
The Mandarin Oriental, Marrakech functions as an 
“oasis”. Surrounded by gardens, with views of the Atlas 
Mountains, it has some of the largest accommodations in the 
city. Exclusivity in a place for those who want 
to step away (but not too far!) from the intense rhythm and return to 
the city in their own time.
105:8:
The Royal Mansour Marrakech 8  belongs, like the one in 
Casablanca, to the king. It is like living in a habitable 
work of art. Intimate, it was literally made by hand; all 
accommodations are private three-story riads, 
tucked into silent courtyards. It is on the list of 
World’s 50 Best Hotels, in 13th place.
105:9:
La Mamounia 9 , also in the ranking (30th place), is 
an absolute icon. Over a hundred years old, it is impossible 
to separate Marrakech from La Mamounia. One of those hotels 
that inhabit the imagination of ten out of ten travelers. 
Legendary gardens, grand atmosphere. Being there is being 
part of a narrative that spans generations.
105:24:
By 4x4, the city falls behind, the horizon 
opens and the world becomes simpler. 
The road leads to a desert of stones, not 
sand. An almost lunar, austere scene; few 
sounds, few colors. The sun sets, the golden light touches 
the stones and everything organizes itself around the moment 
most awaited of the day: in a Berber tent, 
the sunset is celebrated with music, dance, 
tastings and an open fire. The hypnotic experience
105:25:
is signed by SCARABEO CAMP 6 , which understands 
the desert as it is: space and silence.

FROM THE SCENTS OF INCENSE in temples to the aromatic intensity 
of spices in markets, India reveals itself through aromas — and The 
Imperial New Delhi knows this very well. Inspired by the healing philosophy 
of the Mudras, the spa offers personalized holistic sessions. With each 
one, a bespoke aroma is created from the curation of nine 
essential oils suited to all skin types, carefully 
prepared with botanical herbs from the valleys of the Himalayas, ranging from 
botanical aromas to revitalizing infusions such as tangerine and black 
pepper, including the rare oud resin, known in perfumery 
as “liquid gold”. In gastronomy, beyond the brasserie 1911 and the 
sophisticated The Hardinge Bar, the hotel stands out for the legendary Spice 
Route, one of the leading addresses of the local dining scene. With 
nine settings representing the stages of life, the restaurant 
filled with antique pieces offers a sensory and aesthetic immersion. 
The Asian ethnic cuisine menu celebrates everything from warm spices to 
refreshing aromas in its new dishes, which now accompany 
its signature dishes: the black cod marinated in miso and the puff 
pastries filled with duck and shaped into charming 
handbags (a fragrant truffle mayonnaise on the side). 
To learn more about the city’s aromatic identity, the hotel 
also organizes guided visits to iconic places such as the Spice 
Market and Lodhi Garden. Set aside a day for the hotel’s driver 
to take you to Agra to visit the Taj Mahal, the most famous ode to love.

with nature in a project signed by Marcelo Franco,

the tradition of the crustacean accompanied by musical performances
