Carmel Cumbuco Resort takes the experience on the Ceará coast to the next level. Lagoons, coconut groves and dunes form 
the backdrop for the 88 suites, all with a balcony and views of the sea or the pool. No matter where 
the Cumbuco winds take you throughout the day — between kite sessions, buggy adventures on the dunes or jangada 
rides —, the resort’s best can be enjoyed at the end of the afternoon with a special meal. The Churrasco 
ao Mar transforms the sand into an al fresco dining room with a selection of seafood, meats and poultry 
prepared under the starlight. For even more special moments, the Romantic Dinner presents an 
exclusive menu of starter, main course and dessert in a setting with intimate lighting and decor.

made way for the exclusive Carmel Charme 
Resort and its barefoot luxury concept. Today, Praia 
do Barro Preto is home to 35 accommodations facing 
the sea. For those seeking privacy, the 
Premium Bungalow with exclusive pool and whirlpool 
is a winner. On the third floor, the 
two-story Lofts offer a stay with 
stunning views from ample oceanfront 
balconies. At the table (or not!), the resort presents 
the best of Ceará in every meal. Breakfast 
can be served on a floating barge 
over the private pool with fresh ingredients. 
For the most special moments, book the 
tasting menu that unfolds across eight courses to unite 
regional essence with contemporary cuisine. 
And at dusk, how about ending the day with a 
picnic featuring clericot and a sea view?

DURING YOUR STAYS at any resort in the 
Carmel Hotéis group, there is a culinary experience 
that is a must: Pescado Rústico. From the native populations 
of the Ceará coast, who mastered artisanal fishing techniques 
and open-fire cooking, to 
the Portuguese and Africans who brought new 
ingredients and ways of cooking, Ceará’s cuisine 
was shaped by cultural encounters and by the direct 
relationship with the sea and the land — and Pescado Rústico tells 
this story of continuity.
110:3:
Prepared by families who keep alive the recipes 
of their grandparents and great-grandparents, the culinary heritage of this dish 
translates the way of life of coastal communities and their 
deep respect for nature. Caught from jangadas
110:4:
and small boats, the very fresh fish are 
prepared over coals with dry coconut, a traditional technique 
that preserves their flavors and succulent texture. To 
complete the experience, a fisherman shares stories 
of his routine at sea while the coals (re)ignite the 
spirit of the Ceará coast.

118:27: S T A R F I S H  B E A C H E S  |  Z A N Z I B A R
118:28:
Finding starfish during a 
walk on the beach is relatively 
common — but this is an experience 
far more striking. On some beaches 
of the Zanzibar archipelago, bathed 
by the Indian Ocean, the sands can be 
covered with them. The most impressive part? 
They are a very singular vivid red: the 
species, Protoreaster linckii, is found 
only in this ocean and in the Western 
Pacific. The reason for this phenomenon is 
prosaic. It generally results from the combination of 
abundant specimens (a sign of marine 
health) and changes in ocean 
currents. If tides are especially 
strong, they carry them to the beach. An 
image to stay in memory — and one you 
can experience at Mnemba Island, p. 239.
118:40: H I M B A  |  A N G O L A  A N D  N A M I B I A
118:41:
The Himba live in small circular villages 
formed by huts of clay, wood and thatch, 
always built around the sacred fire. And 
it is before that same fire that they communicate 
with the ancestors, asking for protection and balance. 
They are known as “the guardians of the red 
earth,” and their philosophy holds that every 
drop of water and every herd is essential and 
means survival. Turn the page to have 
the author Flávia Pires tell you more about them.
118:44:
The Olympics are yet another 
of the legacies left by 
Greek civilization. And, as one would 
expect, the tradition was born 
as a religious celebration. 
It was around 776 BC in the 
sanctuary-city of Olympia, in 
honor of Zeus, the chief god of 
their cosmology. Since that 
time, the games took place 
every four years and included 
events that endure to this day, 
such as running and discus 
throw (with the difference that 
the athletes representing the 
city-states competed nude; 
did you know that?). The games were 
banned in the 4th century and revived 
in 1896 — to this day, at each 
edition of the Olympic Games, the 
torch is lit there.
119:5: Mosaico | F O C U S  |  P E O P L E
119:8:
By Flávia Pires
T 
he Himba women are the most striking symbol of this people’s bond 
with the land, covering skin and hair with a reddish mixture made 
of ochre powder and animal fat, the otjize. Its application is a daily ritual 
that goes far beyond aesthetics. The pigment protects from the scorching sun, 
wards off insects and, above all, represents the sacred bond with the earth, the 
mother of all. The red tone, color of blood, in Himba tradition also represents 
life and fertility. Their hairstyles, meticulously braided and 
shaped with the same pigment, serve to tell stories, 
marking age, marital status, and even the stage of life each  
woman has reached. Every detail of the body is symbol and memory.

127:17:
CRUISE
E
cuador is a one-of-a-kind destination for 
those seeking nature and adventure. A country 
where you can zipline through the forest “piloting” 
a sky bike, 60 meters above the ground, and fly 
over erupting volcanoes by helicopter. Singular! 
In this country, Galápagos is a must. The best way 
to rediscover the secrets that fascinated Darwin 
more than 190 years ago is aboard the only Relais 
& Châteaux yachts in the archipelago. Origin, Theory 
and Evolve, from Ecoventura, welcome 20 travelers 
each and carry two naturalist guides in the crew. 
They will help you come face to face 
with sea lions, blue-footed boobies, Galápagos 
penguins and other species that exist 
nowhere else. As you sail through islands like 
San Cristóbal and Santa Cruz, prepare for active 
days: hikes, dives, encounters with the 
giant tortoises. Choose itineraries that include 
Floreana, where the tortoises were successfully 
reintroduced after being extinct from the island. Tip from the 
Abercrombie & Kent experts: extend the trip 
to Quito (a stay at Casa Gangotena evokes 
colonial times, in the Historic Center) and Guayaquil 
— stay at Hotel del Parque, an “urban oasis” on the 
banks of the Daule River, set in a late 
19th-century building.

T
he partnership we had all been waiting for has finally arrived! The cruise 
company Crystal Cruises has joined its sister brand Abercrombie 
& Kent, a reference in luxury travel, to launch a 
collection of ten immersive itineraries aboard the Serenity 
and Symphony ships starting in 2026. From a visit to the Vatican before 
opening hours to a sampan ride along the Mekong River, these 
new offerings promise to raise the level of personalization 
on your next cruise to the maximum. Here’s why:
128:10:
ITINERARY: Rome (Italy) › Sorrento (Italy) › Syracuse 
(Italy) › Corfu (Greece) › Kotor (Montenegro) › Zadar 
(Croatia) › Ravenna (Italy) › Venice – Fusina (Italy)
128:11:
PRE-CRUISE STAY IN ITALY: Two nights 
at Anantara Palazzo Naiadi Rome Hotel.
128:12:
OUR PICK: On this inaugural trip, enjoy 
access to the Vatican’s treasures before opening 
hours, when the clavigero unlocks the doors for 
you. Or delve into the architecture of the Basilica of San 
Lorenzo, normally closed to the public.
128:16:
ITINERARY: Valletta (Malta) › Tunis (Tunisia) › Cagliari 
(Italy) › Alicante (Spain) › Valencia (Spain) › 
Mallorca (Spain) › Barcelona (Spain)
128:17:
PRE-CRUISE STAY IN MALTA: Two nights 
at Corinthia Palace Hotel.
128:18:
OUR PICK: Discover the historic attractions 
of Tunis, such as the vibrant souks in the medina and the 
Roman baths of Carthage. Then, relax on 
lounge chairs reserved for you on a beach 
bathed by the warm waters of the Mediterranean.
128:22:
ITINERARY: Bangkok (Thailand) › Koh Kood (Thailand) 
› Phu Quoc (Vietnam) › Ho Chi Minh (Vietnam) › Muara

OUR PICK: (Re)discover the secrets of Ho Chi 
Minh City: choose between a calligraphy workshop 
with a local master or a sampan ride — a traditional 
wooden boat of Southeast Asia — through the 
Mekong River Delta.
128:26:
Limited to 50 travelers, these journeys include two pre-cruise 
nights at five-star hotels, plus tours led 
by A&K local insiders. On board, you’ll have access to VIP 
events and dinners, invitation-only, plus workshops with the 
expedition team and the option to choose your shore excursions. 
Add to that Crystal’s signature nearly one-to-one crew-to-guest 
service ratio, and top-notch dining — 
with the only Nobu at sea (but you already knew that, right?).
128:30:
+ ADVENTURES ON LAND 
The partnership has also given rise to the new Overland 
Adventures: offered on select traditional itineraries, 
such as trips to the Galápagos and 
Alaska, they let you disembark in the 
middle of the cruise to experience a 
multi-night land adventure before reboarding the ship, 
at the next port. Possibilities include visits 
to icons of India, such as the Taj Mahal, and stays in 
Ho Chi Minh and Bangkok.

131:6:
T
he first resort in the Maldives with an artsy proposition — that is the 
essence of JOALI, where nature and design lead the stays 
across the 73 private villas. The art collection spreads across the entire 
island of Muravandhoo, weaving into the landscape a creative 
and playful narrative. Among the highlights are the treehouse by artist Porky 
Hefer shaped like a giant manta ray, “hidden” amid the jungle; the 
coral sculpture by Zemer Peled, made with thousands of porcelain fragments, 
in the wellness center; and the oval lounge-installation by Studio 
Mameluca, on Vandhoo beach. Singular! Surrounded by the serene 
turquoise of Raa atoll, the setting invites you to enjoy the 
outdoors: from wakeboard sessions to rides aboard a dhoni.
131:10:
BRING BACK HOME 
The in-house boutique curates 
fashion items signed by 
designers. Pay attention to the 
capsule collection by Aquazzura: the sandal 
and the white leather clutch, with 
prints of coconut trees and 
turquoise waters, are the result of a 
partnership between the resort and the label’s 
creative director, Edgardo Osorio.
131:14:
T
he history of Plaza Athénée is intertwined with fashion and the trajectory of Christian Dior. In 1946, the designer set up his 
atelier on Avenue Montaigne, a few steps from the Eiffel Tower, to stay close to the hotel — where many of his 
shows and photoshoots were held. Since then, Plaza Athénée has established itself as a meeting point for fashionistas. 
Visual elegance is a mantra: from the contemporary, ethereal design of the Dior Spa (the brand’s first in Europe!) 
to the authentic art déco style of Relais Plaza, a brasserie that reinterprets French cuisine, every space conveys 
sophistication. Perfect for celebrations, the Haute Couture ballroom delivers a timeless setting, with golden details, 
seven chandeliers and a gold-leafed ceiling.
131:18:
BRING BACK HOME 
No, it’s not Dior… it’s the hotel’s 
mode bébé. Adorable! The 
100% cotton bib will help 
protect the look of the petit 
gastronomes at mealtimes, and it’s 
hard to resist the hotel mascot, 
the charming Gustave, in his 
plush version. Handmade 
in Italy, the red teddy bear 
has become a symbol of Plaza 
Athénée among children.
132:4:
I
n the heart of Florida, this is the place to be to combine sunny days with golf. Among the accommodations, standouts include 
the Cottages, two-bedroom cabins, and The Champion Suite, with three balconies and views of The Fazio 
golf course. This course, designed in honor of five-time champion Walter Hagen, is one of the resort’s five and 
is especially challenging for low-handicap golfers (watch out for hole 4, par 5!). Off the greens, there’s no shortage of options 
to recharge: Butcher’s Club is a modern steakhouse with succulent cuts by Jeremy Ford (Michelin-starred 
chef and winner of a Top Chef season) and the sophisticated Sushi Boū draws inspiration from 1920s speakeasies to 
serve an omakase experience for 12 people.
132:7:

With exclusive 
pieces signed by the PGA, 
the resort shop is a must to 
keep the style on and 
off the course: in addition 
to polos and plush toys, 
limited edition items — 
such as the microfiber towel 
and the commemorative 
golf ball set — round out the collection.

135:6:
T 
he arrival of the 60th anniversary of Barbadian 
independence is turning the 
island into one of the liveliest destinations in the Caribbean. 
Everything will converge on November 30, 
when the traditional parade takes over the 
Bridgetown racecourse while blue and 
yellow lights — a reference to the colors of the national 
flag — illuminate public buildings. Since for 
us everything is an excuse to travel, we've organized here 
some of our favorite spots for you to visit 
the island in this year of celebration.
135:8:
There are two absolute references on the island. The 
iconic Sandy Lane, with its unmistakable 
charm in pink tones on the edge of the Platinum 
Coast, and the elegant Fairmont Royal Pavilion. 
Two classics that span generations 
while keeping their relevance, impeccable service and 
a sense of place that, on its own, already justifies 
the trip. But we always think it’s worth 
exploring more great options — how about these?
135:9:
1  THE HOUSE, BARBADOS,  
AN AUTOGRAPH COLLECTION
ST. JAMES, BARBADOS
The hotel, with its exclusive atmosphere, sits 
amid the Platinum Coast — the “stronghold” of the 
most famous resorts. With an adults-only 
concept and only 39 accommodations (some 
with private plunge pool), The House elevates the 
stay experience with a complimentary massage 
to help with jet lag, daily 
yoga sessions and champagne breakfast. The 
décor leans on a palette of cool tones, with 
details in mahogany and white granite.
135:10:
2  O2 BEACH CLUB & SPA
CHRIST CHURCH, BARBADOS
On the south coast — great for snorkeling on reefs 
—, it is the only one with a hammam experience. The 
spa also offers body rituals with products by the 
vegan brand OSEA. Three pools (including a 
river pool for adults only) and the beachfront 
location put you in constant contact 
with the water. Five restaurants propose 
diverse cuisines; try 
Oro, with 260° views of the surroundings and 
European gastronomy with a Caribbean twist.
135:12:
Another super-exclusive way to experience Barbados 
is by booking a villa. On the island, there are options that 
combine colonial-inspired architecture, 
beach access and landscaped gardens — plus 
included private chef and butler services 
to make your days even more comfortable.
135:14:
• CROP OVER FESTIVAL: Carnival-style 
revelry recalls the enslaved peoples 
and the celebrations 
related to the end of the sugarcane 
harvests.
135:16:
• FOOD & RUM FESTIVAL: a festival 
dedicated to typical bajan cuisine and 
its blend of flavors: African, 
British, Indian, Portuguese...
135:18:
• MAIN INDEPENDENCE PARADE: 
marching bands and military 
units parade in tribute 
to the island’s independence after 
British colonization.
135:31:
Pendry Barbados: hasn’t 
opened yet and we already love it
